17-19-2.data_last_update: Last updated on 2026/04/17. Data for further reporting periods will be added as soon as it becomes available.
17-19-2.page_content: In Germany, the Civil Status Act (Personenstandsgesetz, PStG) regulates the registration of births and deaths. The birth of a child must be reported to the relevant registry office within one week. This rule applies to all children born in Germany. A death must be reported no later than the third working day after the death.

6-5-2.page_content: The integrated management of rivers and lakes in transboundary basin areas is organised by International River Basin Commissions. The work of the commissions is based on international agreements. Germany is a member of eight international river basin commissions: Rhine, Danube, Elbe, Oder, Meuse, Moselle, Saar and Lake Constance. This means that operational agreements for water cooperation apply to 100% of Germany's transboundary basin areas.

13-1-2.indicator_name: Number of countries that adopt and implement national disaster risk reduction strategies in line with the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction 2015-2030
13-1-2.target_name: Strengthen resilience and adaptive capacity to climate-related hazards and natural disasters in all countries
13-1-2.un_designated_tier_url_alert: der Statistischen Division der Vereinten Nationen
13-1-2.un_custodian_agency: United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction (UNDRR)
13-1-2.goal_meta_link_alert: the Statistical Devision of the UN
13-1-2.source_organisation_1: Federal Office of Civil Protection and Disaster Assistance
13-1-2.source_url_text_1: German Strategy for Strengthening Resilience to Disasters
13-1-2.source_url_alert_1: the Federal Office of Civil Protection and Disaster Assistance
13-1-2.page_content:
In March 2015, the Federal Republic of Germany committed itself to „Implementing the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (2015-2030)“ of the United Nations (UN). The Federal Office for Civil Protection and Disaster Assistance (Bundesamt für Bevölkerungsschutz und Katastrophenhilfe, BBK) is the national focal point responsible for implementing the Sendai Framework.

On 13 July 2022, the Federal Government adopted the German Strategy for Strengthening Resilience to Disasters (Resilience Strategy). The aim of this strategy is to improve the protection of people and their livelihoods and to strengthen the community's resilience and adaptability to disasters in the long term.

footer_fields.value:
• Number of victims per 100,000 inhabitants (V/100,000): Number of victims per 100,000 inhabitants, based on the population on 31 December of the previous year. Own calculation based on the 2022 census.
• The data is based on a special evaluation and is not publicly available.
• For victims recorded in the reporting year, the year in which the offence was committed may also be prior to the reporting year (initial statistics). Persons who were recorded as victims multiple times in the reporting year are counted multiple times accordingly.
• Due to methodological changes, data is only available from 2018 onwards. For further information, see ‘3. Description of the data’ in the national metadata.

Data source: German Environment Agency
Geographic coverage: Germany
Unit of measurement: Number, DALYs
Notes: The upper and lower estimator (confidence interval) represent the uncertainty of the calculation based on the relative risk.
Number of attributable deaths: Number of deaths that can be statistically attributed to long-term exposure to particulate matter (PM2,5).
Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs): Number of healthy life years lost due to death and morbidity caused by diseases that can be statistically attributed to long-term exposure to fine particulate matter (PM2,5).

footer_fields.series: Progress toward productive and sustainable agriculture (proxy)
footer_fields.label: Notes
footer_fields.value:
• Data is only available from 2015.
• Assessment of the current status:
• 1 to <1.5: Very far.
• 1.5 to <2.5: Far.
• 2.5 to <3.5: At a moderate distance.
• 3.5 to <4.5: Close.
• 4.5 to 5: Productive and sustainable agriculture already achieved.
• Assessment of the trend:
• 1 to <1.5: Deterioration.
• 1.5 to <2.5: Slight deterioration.
• 2.5 to <3.5: No improvement.
• 3.5 to <4.5: Slight improvement.
• 4.5 to 5: Improvement.

10-7-4.data_footnotes:
The indicator measures the proportion of German nationals who are refugees abroad per 100,000 inhabitants of Germany rather than the number of refugees living in Germany.
• For 2010, the population was calculated backwards using the 2011 census and migration, birth and death statistics.
• Refugee: A person who is outside the country of his or her nationality or permanent residence and who, owing to his or her race, religion, nationality, membership of a particular social group or political opinion, has a well-founded fear of being persecuted and is unable to avail himself or herself of the protection of that country or to return there owing to that fear of persecution (Article 1 of the Geneva Convention).

5-c-1.target_name: Adopt and strengthen sound policies and enforceable legislation for the promotion of gender equality and the empowerment of all women and girls at all levels

5-c-1.source_url_alert_2: the Federal Ministry of Justice and the Federal Office of Justice and Consumer Protection
5-c-1.page_content:
Article 3 of the Basic Law for the Federal Republic of Germany (Grundgesetz, GG) guarantees equal rights for women and men.

Federal ministries and federal authorities are obliged to make their spending policies gender-sensitive. Based on this mainstreaming approach, funds for promoting gender equality and women's self-determination are provided for in almost all budget items of the Federal Government.

The instrument of gender budgeting has not yet been applied in the federal budget, but is already being used in individual Länder.

3-8-1.comparison_sdg: Information is provided on the respective pages of the SDG indicators.
3-8-1.target_name: Achieve universal health coverage, including financial risk protection, access to quality essential health-care services and access to safe, effective, quality and affordable essential medicines and vaccines for all
3-8-1.un_designated_tier_url_alert: der Statistischen Division der Vereinten Nationen
3-8-1.goal_meta_link_alert: the Statistical Devision of the UN
3-8-1.page_content:
Coverage of essential health services Related SDG indicator
I. Reproductive, maternal, newborn and child health
1. Family planning:
Percentage of women of reproductive age (15-49 years) who have their need for family planning satisfied with modern methods 		Not available
2. Pregnancy care:
Percentage of women aged 15-49 years with a live birth in a given time period who received antenatal care four or more times 		Not available
3. Child immunization:
Percentage of infants receiving three doses of diphtheria-tetanus-pertussis containing vaccine 		3.b.1 Proportion of the target population covered by all vaccines included in their national programme
4. Child treatment:
Percentage of children younger than 5 years with symptoms of acute respiratory infection (cough and fast or difficult breathing due to a problem in the chest and not due to a blocked nose only) in the 2 weeks preceding the survey for whom advice or treatment was sought from a health facility or provider 		Not available
II. Infectious diseases
5. Tuberculosis:
Percentage of incident TB cases that are detected and treated 		Not available
6. HIV/AIDS:
Percentage of adults and children living with HIV currently receiving antiretroviral therapy 		Not available
7. Malaria:
Percentage of population in malaria-endemic areas who slept under an insecticide-treated net the previous night [only for countries with high malaria burden] 		Not applicable
8. Water, sanitation and hygiene:
Percentage of population using at least basic sanitation services 		6.2.1 Proportion of population using (a) safely managed sanitation services and (b) a hand-washing facility with soap and water
III. Noncommunicable diseases
9. Hypertension:
Coverage of treatment (taking medicine) for hypertension among adults aged 30-79 years with hypertension (age-standardized estimate) (%) 		Not available
10. Diabetes:
Coverage of treatment (taking medication) for diabetes among adults aged 30 years and over with diabetes (age-standardized estimate) (%) 		Not available
11. Tobacco:
Age-standardized prevalence of adults (≥15 years) currently using any tobacco product (smoked and/or smokeless tobacco) on a daily or non-daily basis 		3.a.1 Age-standardized prevalence of current tobacco use among persons aged 15 years and older
IV. Service capacity and access
12. Hospital access:
Hospital beds density, relative to a maximum threshold of 18 per 10,000 population 		Not available
13. Health workforce:
Health workers (medical doctors, nursing and midwifery personnel) per capita, relative to a combined maximum threshold 		3.c.1 Health worker density and distribution
14. Health security:
International Health Regulations (IHR) core capacity index, which is the average percentage of attributes of 13 core capacities that have been attained 		3.d.1 International Health Regulations (IHR) capacity and health emergency preparedness

1-5-4.page_content: Disaster control in Germany is based on federal and state regulations. All 16 Länder have their own Civil Protection Laws, meaning that 100% of municipalities in Germany are subject to these legal requirements.

Not applicable
This indicator is marked as not applicable for the provision of national time series. footer_fields.label: Notes
footer_fields.value:
• Number of victims per 100,000 inhabitants (V/100,000): Number of victims per 100,000 inhabitants, based on the population on 31 December of the previous year. Up to and including 2012, the data is based on the 1987 census (Federal Republic of Germany) and the population register as of 3 October 1990 (German Democratic Republic); for the years 2013 to 2023, it is based on the 2011 census, and from the 2024 onwards, it is based on the 2022 census.
• For victims recorded in the reporting year, the year in which the offence was committed may also be prior to the reporting year (initial statistics). Persons who were recorded as victims multiple times in the reporting year are counted multiple times accordingly.
• Due to methodological changes, data from 2017 onwards is only comparable with previous years to a limited extent. For further information, see ‘1. General information on the time series’ in the national metadata.

footer_fields.label: Notes
footer_fields.value:
• The calculation is based on the retroactively calculated average population. For 2010, this was determined on the basis of the 2011 census and migration, birth and death statistics; for 2012 to 2021, it was determined analogously on the basis of the 2022 census.
• For victims recorded in the reporting year, the year in which the offence was committed may also be prior to the reporting year (initial statistics). Persons who were recorded as victims multiple times in the reporting year are counted multiple times accordingly.
• Due to methodological changes, data from 2017 onwards is only comparable with previous years to a limited extent. For further information, see ‘1. General information on the time series’ in the national metadata.

footer_fields.label: Notes
footer_fields.value:

• Due to methodological changes, data from 2017 onwards are only comparable with previous years to a limited extent. For further information, see ‘1. General information on the time series’ in the national metadata.

17-14-1.page_content: In April 2002, the Federal Government published its first National Strategy for Sustainable Development, which has been continuously developed since then. The Federal Government submits a progress report on its implementation every four years. Since 2015, the 2030 Agenda, with its 17 global Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), has formed the basis of the Federal Government’s sustainability policy. Building on this, the Federal Cabinet approved the New Version 2016 on 11 January 2017. This was followed by the 2021 Update on 10 March 2021 and, the current 2025 Update on 29 January 2025. According to UN metadata for SDG indicator 17.14.1, Germany has achieved a score of "73.96".

1-4-1.goal_meta_link_alert: the Statistical Devision of the UN
1-4-1.page_content:
SDG indicator 1.4.1 is a dashboard based on existing SDG indicators that maps various aspects of basic services.


Access to Related SDG indicator
Basic drinking water services 6.1.1 Proportion of population using safely managed drinking water services
Basic sanitation services 6.2.1 Proportion of population using (a) safely managed sanitation services and (b) a hand-washing facility with soap and water
Basic hygiene services 6.2.1 Proportion of population using (a) safely managed sanitation services and (b) a hand-washing facility with soap and water
Waste collection 11.6.1 Proportion of municipal solid waste collected and managed in controlled facilities out of total municipal waste generated, by cities
Mobility and transport 9.1.1 Proportion of the rural population who live within 2 km of an all-season road
11.2.1 Proportion of population that has convenient access to public transport, by sex, age and persons with disabilities
Modern energy 7.1.1 Percentage of population with access to electricity
7.1.2 Percentage of population with primary reliance on clean fuels and technology
Information and communication technology 5.b.1 Proportion of individuals who own a mobile telephone, by sex
9.c.1 Proportion of population covered by a mobile network, by technology
Education 4.1.1 Proportion of children and young people (a) in grades 2/3; (b) at the end of primary; and (c) at the end of lower secondary achieving at least a minimum proficiency level in (i) reading and (ii) mathematics, by sex
Health 3.7.1 Proportion of women of reproductive age (aged 15–49 years) who have their need for family planning satisfied with modern methodsNot available

11-b-1.indicator_name: Number of countries that adopt and implement national disaster risk reduction strategies in line with the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction 2015–2030
11-b-1.target_name: By 2020, substantially increase the number of cities and human settlements adopting and implementing integrated policies and plans towards inclusion, resource efficiency, mitigation and adaptation to climate change, resilience to disasters, and develop and implement, in line with the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction 2015-2030, holistic disaster risk management at all levels
11-b-1.un_designated_tier_url_alert: der Statistischen Division der Vereinten Nationen
11-b-1.un_custodian_agency: United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction (UNDRR)
11-b-1.goal_meta_link_alert: the Statistical Devision of the UN
11-b-1.source_organisation_1: Federal Office of Civil Protection and Disaster Assistance
11-b-1.source_url_text_1: German Strategy for Strengthening Resilience to Disasters
11-b-1.source_url_alert_1: the Federal Office of Civil Protection and Disaster Assistance
11-b-1.page_content:
In March 2015, the Federal Republic of Germany committed itself to „Implementing the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (2015-2030)“ of the United Nations (UN). The Federal Office for Civil Protection and Disaster Assistance (Bundesamt für Bevölkerungsschutz und Katastrophenhilfe, BBK) is the national focal point responsible for implementing the Sendai Framework.

On 13 July 2022, the Federal Government adopted the German Strategy for Strengthening Resilience to Disasters (Resilience Strategy). The aim of this strategy is to improve the protection of people and their livelihoods and to strengthen the community's resilience and adaptability to disasters in the long term.

6-1-1.data_last_update: Last updated on 2024/08/12. Data for further reporting periods will be added as soon as it becomes available.
6-1-1.page_content: The legal basis for water supply in Germany is regulated by federal level in the Federal Water Resources Act (Wasserhaushaltsgesetz, WHG) and at the state level in the respective Water Laws of the Länder. Water supply is a public service and is usually provided by local authorities. The obligation to connect to and use the public water supply is intended to ensure a secure supply of drinking water to the population and to prevent or restrict the private use of groundwater. Over 99% of the population in Germany is connected to central drinking water distribution networks.

footer_fields.label: Notes
footer_fields.value:
• The calculation is based on the retoactively calculated average population. For 2010, this was determined on the basis of the 2011 census and migration, birth and death statistics; for 2012 to 2021, ist was determined analogously on the basis of the 2022 census.
• For victims recorded in the reporting year, the year in which the offence was committed may also be prior to the reporting year (initial statistics). Persons who were recorded as victims multiple times in the reporting year are counted multiple times accordingly.
• Victims of sexual harassment: Due to methodological changes, data from 2021 onwards are only comparable with previous years to a limited extent. For further information, see ‘1. General information on the time series’ in the national metadata.

footer_fields.label: Notes
footer_fields.value:
• Number of victims per 100,000 inhabitants (V/100,000): Number of victims per 100,000 inhabitants, based on the population on 31 December of the previous year. Up to and including 2012, the data is based on the 1987 census (Federal Republic of Germany) and the population register as of 3 October 1990 (German Democratic Republic); for the years 2013 to 2023, it is based on the 2011 census, and from 2024 onwards, it is based on the 2022 census.
• For victims recorded in the reporting year, the year in which the offence was committed may also be prior to the reporting year (initial statistics). Persons who were recorded as victims multiple times in the reporting year are counted multiple times accordingly.
• Victims of sexual harassment: Due to methodological changes, data from 2021 onwards are only comparable with previous years to a limited extent. For further information, see ‘1. General information on the time series’ in the national metadata.

footer_fields.label: Notes
footer_fields.value:
• For victims recorded in the reporting year, the year in which the offence was committed may also be prior to the reporting year (initial statistics). Persons who were recorded as victims multiple times in the reporting year are counted multiple times accordingly.
• Due to methodological changes, data from 2021 onwards are only comparable with previous years to a limited extent. For further information, see ‘1. General information on the time series’ in the national metadata.

footer_fields.label: Notes
footer_fields.value:
• The calculation is based on the retroactively calculated average population. For 2010, this was determined on the basis of the 2011 census and migration, birth and death statistics; for 2012 to 2021, it was determined analogously on the basis of the 2022 census.
• For victims recorded in the reporting year, the year in which the offence was committed may also be prior to the reporting year (initial statistics). Persons who were recorded as victims multiple times in the reporting year are counted multiple times accordingly.

footer_fields.label: Notes
footer_fields.value:
• Number of victims per 100,000 inhabitants (V/100,000): Number of victims per 100,000 inhabitants, based on the population on 31 December of the previous year. Up to and including 2012, the data is based on the 1987 census (Federal Republic of Germany) and the population register as of 3 October 1990 (German Democratic Republic); for the years 2013 to 2023, it is based on the 2011 census, and from 2024 onwards, it is based on the 2022 census.
• For victims recorded in the reporting year, the year in which the offence was committed may also be prior to the reporting year (initial statistics). Persons who were recorded as victims multiple times in the reporting year are counted multiple times accordingly.

footer_fields.label: Note
footer_fields.value: For victims recorded in the reporting year, the year in which the offence was committed may also be prior to the reporting year (initial statistics). Persons who were recorded as victims multiple times in the reporting year are counted multiple times accordingly.

16-6-1.target_name: Develop effective, accountable and transparent institutions at all levels
16-6-1.un_designated_tier_url_alert: der Statistischen Division der Vereinten Nationen
16-6-1.goal_meta_link_alert: the Statistical Devision of the UN

16-6-1.data_footnotes:
Primary government expenditures: Actual budget.
• Original approved budget: Target budget.
• Scoring on a four-level ordinal scale based on primary government expenditures as a proportion of original approved budget:
• A: Scoring was between 95 % and 105 % in at least two of the last three years.
• B: Scoring was between 90 % and 110 % in at least two of the last three years.
• C: Scoring was between 85 % and 115 % in at least two of the last three years.
• D: Performance is less than required for a C score.

8-9-2.data_footnotes:
Cultural activities and Sports and Recreational activities: Data is only available from 2021.
• All other economic activities: Data is only available from 2020.
• Employed persons: Total number of working proprietors, unpaid family workers and employees.
• Employees (wage and salary earners): Persons who, on the reference date of 30 September of the reporting year, were in an employment or comparable professional relationship with the survey unit and received remuneration from it in the form of wages, salaries, bonuses, commissions, training benefits, or benefits in kind.
• Full-time equivalents: Total number of contractually agreed weekly working hours of all employees, divided by the regular weekly working hours of a full-time employee applicable in the survey unit or for the respective occupational group.

6-b-1.page_content: The Water Framework Directive came into force in December 2000. Its aim is to establish a coherent framework for the protection of inland surface waters, transitional and coastal waters, and groundwater. At national level, the directive is implemented in the Water Resources Act (Wasserhaushaltsgesetz, WHG), the Water Laws of the Länder and through decrees and Länder ordinances.

footer_fields.series: Animal genetic resources
footer_fields.label: Notes
footer_fields.value:
• Animal Genetic Resources (TGR): Material from agricultural and food animals (livestock) that contains functional heritable units. Currently limited to cattle, pigs, sheep, goats, horses, rabbits, chickens, geese, ducks, turkeys, and pigeons.
• The data is only available from 2017.

14-2-1.page_content: In 2006, the Federal Cabinet adopted the National Strategy for Integrated Coastal Zone Management (ICZM) in Germany. At the end of 2009, the federal spatial planning regulations for the Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) in the North Sea and Baltic Sea came into force. 15-8-1.embedded_feature_html:
Indicator 15.8.1

Proportion of countries adopting relevant national legislation and adequately resourcing the prevention or control of invasive alien species

1. International conventions and protocols

1.1 Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD)

  • Official website
  • Full text of the convention
  • National contact point
  • National implementation reports
  • Further information on the website of the Federal Agency for Nature Conservation (BfN) (only available in German)

1.2 Cartagena Protocol on Biosafety to the Convention on Biological Diversity (Cartagena Protocol)

  • Official website
  • Full text of the convention
  • National contact point
  • National implementation reports

1.3 Convention on the Conservation of Migratory Species of Wild Animals (Bonn Convention, CMS)

  • Official website
  • Full text of the convention
  • National contact point
  • National implementation reports
  • Further information on the website of the Federal Ministry for the Environment, Climate Action, Nature Conservation and Nuclear Safety (BMUKN)
  • Further information on the website of the Federal Agency for Nature Conservation (BfN) (only available in German)

1.4 Convention on Wetlands of International Importance especially as Waterfowl Habitat (Ramsar Convention)

  • Official website
  • Full text of the convention
  • National contact point
  • National implementation reports
  • Further information on the website of the Federal Agency for Nature Conservation (BfN) (only available in German)

1.5 Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora (CITES)

  • Official website
  • Full text of the convention
  • National contact point
  • National implementation reports
  • Further information on the website of the Federal Ministry for the Environment, Climate Action, Nature Conservation and Nuclear Safety (BMUKN)
  • Further information on the website of the Federal Agency for Nature Conservation (BfN) (only available in German)

1.6 Convention concerning the Protection of the World Cultural and Natural Heritage (World Heritage Convention)

  • Official website
  • Full text of the convention
  • National contact point
  • National implementation reports
  • Further information on the website of the Federal Agency for Nature Conservation (BfN) (only available in German)

1.7 International Convention for the Control and Management of Ships' Ballast Water and Sediments (Ballast Water Management Convention, BWM)

  • Official website
  • Full text of the convention
  • National contact point
  • National implementation reports

1.8 International Plant Protection Convention (IPPC)

  • Official website
  • Full text of the convention
  • National contact point
  • National implementation reports

1.9 Agreement on the Application of Sanitary and Phytosanitary Measures (SPS Agreement)

  • Official website
  • Full text of the convention
  • National contact point
  • National implementation reports

1.10 World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH)

  • Official website
  • Codes and manuals
  • National contact point
  • National implementation reports (only available in German)

1.11 Convention on the Conservation of European Wildlife and Natural Habitats (Bern Convention)

  • Official website
  • Full text of the convention
  • National contact point
  • National implementation reports
  • Further information on the website of the Federal Ministry for the Environment, Climate Action, Nature Conservation and Nuclear Safety (BMUKN)
  • Further information on the website of the Federal Agency for Nature Conservation (BfN) (only available in German)

2. European regulations

  • Council Directive 92/43/EEC of 21 May 1992 on the conservation of natural habitats and of wild fauna and flora (FFH-Directive)
  • Directive 2009/147/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 30 November 2009 on the conservation of wild birds

3. National regulations (only available in German)

3.1 Nature conservation and species protection

  • Federal Nature Conservation Act (Gesetz ueber Naturschutz und Landschaftspflege, BNatSchG)
  • Federal Species Protection Ordinance (Verordnung zum Schutz wild lebender Tier- und Pflanzenarten, BArtSchV)

3.2 Plants and seeds

  • Plant Protection Act (Gesetz zum Schutz der Kulturpflanzen, PflSchG)
  • Seed Regulation (Verordnung ueber den Verkehr mit Saatgut landwirtschaftlicher Arten und von Gemuesearten, SaatV)
  • Plant Variety Protection Act (Sortenschutzgesetz, SortSchG)

3.3 Animals and animal health

  • Animal Diseases Act (Gesetz zur Vorbeugung vor und Bekaempfung von Tierseuchen, TierGesG)
  • Animal Welfare Act (Tierschutzgesetz, TierSchG)
  • Federal Hunting Act (Bundesjagdgesetz, BJagdG)

3.4 Other relevant laws

  • Federal Forest Act (Gesetz zur Erhaltung des Waldes und zur Foerderung der Forstwirtschaft, BWaldG)

3-b-3.target_name: Support the research and development of vaccines and medicines for the communicable and non-communicable diseases that primarily affect developing countries, provide access to affordable essential medicines and vaccines, in accordance with the Doha Declaration on the TRIPS Agreement and Public Health, which affirms the right of developing countries to use to the full the provisions in the Agreement on Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights regarding flexibilities to protect public health, and, in particular, provide access to medicines for all
3-b-3.un_designated_tier_url_alert: der Statistischen Division der Vereinten Nationen
3-b-3.goal_meta_link_alert: the Statistical Devision of the UN
3-b-3.page_content:
Indicator Link
1. Prevalence of cervical cancer screening among women aged 30-49 years (%) Use of cancer screening services provided by statutory health insurance (SHI) (only available in German)
2. Women accessing antenatal care (ANC) services who were tested for syphilis (%), reported SurvStat@RKI 2.0: Web-based query on data reported under the German 'Protection against Infection Act'
3. New cases tested for RR-/MDR-TB (%) SurvStat@RKI 2.0: Web-based query on data reported under the German 'Protection against Infection Act'
4. Tuberculosis treatment coverage Not available
5. Estimated antiretroviral therapy coverage among people living with HIV (%) Not available
6. Antenatal care attendees positive for syphilis who received treatment (%), reported SurvStat@RKI 2.0: Web-based query on data reported under the German 'Protection against Infection Act'
7. Tuberculosis effective treatment coverage Not available
8. Women of reproductive age (aged 15-49 years) who have their need for family planning satisfied with modern methods (%) Not available
9. Measles-containing-vaccine second-dose (MCV2) immunization coverage by the nationally recommended age (%) Immunization coverage for infants, children and adolescents: Measles (only available in German)
10. Pneumococcal conjugate vaccines (PCV3) immunization coverage among 1-year-olds (%) Immunization coverage for infants, children and adolescents: Pneumococcal disease (only available in German)
11. HPV immunization coverage estimates among primary target cohort (9-14 years old girls) (%) Immunization coverage for infants, children and adolescents: Human papillomavirus (HPV) (only available in German)
12. Diphtheria tetanus toxoid and pertussis (DTP3) immunization coverage among 1-year-olds (%) Immunization coverage for infants, children and adolescents: Diphtheria tetanus toxoid and pertussis (only available in German)
13. Hepatitis B (HepB3) immunization coverage among 1-year-olds (%) Immunization coverage for infants, children and adolescents: Hepatitis B (only available in German)
14. Neonates protected at birth against neonatal tetanus (PAB) (%) The indicator “Neonates protected at birth against neonatal tetanus (PAB) (%)” describes the proportion of newborns who have adequate protection against tetanus based on their mother’s vaccination status at the time of birth.
No specific, regularly collected data on the tetanus vaccination status of pregnant women is available for Germany. However, given the generally high vaccination rates in the population and the elimination of neonatal tetanus, a high level of protection can be assumed.
This is attributable in particular to the following conditions:
  • Births take place almost exclusively under hygienic conditions.
  • Tetanus occurs only very rarely.
  • Vaccination programs are established nationwide.
15. Hib (Hib3) immunization coverage among 1-year-olds (%) Immunization coverage for infants, children and adolescents: Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) (only available in German)
16. Polio (Pol3) immunization coverage among 1-year-olds (%) Immunization coverage for infants, children and adolescents: Polio (only available in German)
17. Measles-containing-vaccine first-dose (MCV1) immunization coverage by the nationally recommended age (%) Immunization coverage for infants, children and adolescents: Measles (only available in German)
18. Population with access to an insecticide-treated bed net (ITN) for malaria protection (%) Not applicable
19. Prevalence of met need of assistive products (%) Reports on medical aids and therapeutic devices under the statutory health insurance scheme (SHI) (only available in German)

17-11-1.page_content:
Not applicable
This indicator is marked as not applicable for the provision of national time series. Possible reasons are: - The indicator is a global indicator that is to say the provision of national data for this indicator would not be meaningful. - The indicator or the corresponding target refers to a specific group of countries (for example small island developing states), to which Germany does not belong.

1-5-3.indicator_name: Number of countries that adopt and implement national disaster risk reduction strategies in line with the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction 2015–2030
1-5-3.target_name: By 2030, build the resilience of the poor and those in vulnerable situations and reduce their exposure and vulnerability to climate-related extreme events and other economic, social and environmental shocks and disasters
1-5-3.un_designated_tier_url_alert: der Statistischen Division der Vereinten Nationen
1-5-3.un_custodian_agency: United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction (UNDRR)
1-5-3.goal_meta_link_alert: the Statistical Devision of the UN
1-5-3.source_organisation_1: Federal Office of Civil Protection and Disaster Assistance
1-5-3.source_url_text_1: German Strategy for Strengthening Resilience to Disasters
1-5-3.source_url_alert_1: the Federal Office of Civil Protection and Disaster Assistance
1-5-3.page_content:
In March 2015, the Federal Republic of Germany committed itself to „Implementing the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (2015-2030)“ of the United Nations (UN). The Federal Office for Civil Protection and Disaster Assistance (Bundesamt für Bevölkerungsschutz und Katastrophenhilfe, BBK) is the national focal point responsible for implementing the Sendai Framework.

On 13 July 2022, the Federal Government adopted the German Strategy for Strengthening Resilience to Disasters (Resilience Strategy). The aim of this strategy is to improve the protection of people and their livelihoods and to strengthen the community's resilience and adaptability to disasters in the long term.
